title: Pediatric oxygen therapy
executiveSummary: Titrate oxygen according to age and disease, monitoring SpO₂ and signs of respiratory fatigue; escalate the device if there is insufficient response.
body:
## Steps to perform

1. Verify the medical prescription and the SpO₂ target according to age, disease, and comorbidities.
2. Choose the appropriate device based on severity (nasal cannula, simple facemask, non-rebreather mask, HFNC, NIV/MV).
3. Apply the device ensuring mucosal humidification when appropriate; secure it without compressing skin or cartilage.
4. Set the flow rate according to the device and weight (low-flow nasal cannula: 0.5–2 L/min in infants; 1–4 L/min in children).
5. Establish continuous monitoring of SpO₂ along with RR and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, retractions, grunting).
6. Reassess response every 30–60 minutes (or continuously in ICU); document the device, flow, and achieved SpO₂.
7. Escalate respiratory support if SpO₂ remains below target despite maximal oxygen delivery with the current device; notify immediately.
8. Notify immediately if there is increasing work of breathing, apnea, cyanosis, new-onset agitation or lethargy, bradycardia, or hypotension.
9. Document the device, flow, approximate FiO₂, and clinical response every shift.

## Scientific rationale

### Indications

- Hypoxemia (SpO₂ below age-appropriate target).
- Moderate-to-severe respiratory distress.
- Shock with hypoperfusion.
- Sepsis, bronchiolitis, pneumonia, or severe asthma.

### Target SpO₂ (general reference)

| Situation | Target SpO₂ |
| --- | --- |
| Infant/child without congenital heart disease | 94–98% |
| Premature infant / bronchopulmonary dysplasia | Per neonatal protocol (e.g., 90–95%) |
| Resolving asthma exacerbation | ≥ 94% |

Adjust targets according to prescription and comorbidities.

### Devices (typical guidance)

| Device | Guide flow rate | Approximate FiO₂ |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Low-flow nasal cannula | 0.5–2 L/min (infant) / 1–4 L/min (child) | 24–40% |
| Simple facemask | 5–10 L/min | 40–60% |
| Non-rebreather (reservoir) mask | 10–15 L/min | 60–90% |
| HFNC | Per weight and ICU protocol | Titrated |
| NIV / MV | Per prescription | — |

> **Source:** World Health Organization. Oxygen therapy for children — WHO guidelines.

### Red flags

- Increasing work of breathing, apnea, or cyanosis.
- New agitation or lethargy.
- SpO₂ persistently below target despite maximal oxygen with current device.
- Bradycardia or hypotension.

bibliography.citation: American Academy of Pediatrics. Bronchiolitis Clinical Practice Guideline. Pediatrics. 2014 (actualizaciones AAP).
Weiss SL, et al. Surviving Sepsis Campaign Pediatric Guidelines 2026.
SATI — Comité de Neumonología Crítica. Soporte respiratorio.
World Health Organization. Oxygen therapy for children — WHO guidelines.
